original_hypothesis.title: Single Broken Genesis Pool DoS Blocks All Epoch Flip Operations
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: checkAtEpochFlip() and checkBeforeEpochFlip() iterate over all liveNativeTokens and make external calls for each pool. If any single pool's external call reverts (e.g., a malicious token that reverts on transfer, or a broken gauge), the entire transaction reverts, blocking all other pools from transitioning. The pre_epoch_period update in checkBeforeEpochFlip only occurs after all iterations, so it remains stale on revert.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: No try-catch wraps the per-pool operations. A single reverted call to pairFactory.setGenesisStatus, IGenesisPool.setPoolStatus, or addLiquidity blocks the entire batch. This creates a permanent DoS where the failing pool prevents all other pools from launching or transitioning.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Loop over liveNativeTokens with external calls and no try-catch; single revert blocks entire function

preconditions: At least one genesis pool in liveNativeTokens has a token or external dependency that reverts
The reverting pool cannot be manually removed before the next epoch

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No try-catch around per-pool external calls in batch processing loop
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Create a genesis pool with a token whose transfer reverts under certain conditions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Block all competing pool launches to gain market advantage or grief depositors

economic_questions: Can governance manually remove the problematic pool to unblock others?
What types of token behavior trigger reverts in the launch flow?
How long can the DoS persist before recovery?
validation_requirements: Create a genesis pool with a reverting token and verify it blocks epoch flip
Test if rejectGenesisPool can remove the offending pool
Verify pre_epoch_period remains stale after failed checkBeforeEpochFlip

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of creating a genesis pool with a malicious token + whitelist requirement
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect - blocks competing launches; depositor funds stuck
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Griefing attack - no direct profit but significant harm

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Minimal - just enough to create genesis pool
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Before epoch flip when multiple pools are pending
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All pending genesis pool launches indefinitely delayed

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All depositors in pending genesis pools
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of all deposits across all pending genesis pools

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Number of live pools, recovery mechanisms available
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify no try-catch and revert propagation

validation_result.mechanism: DoS via unbounded batch external calls without try-catch
validation_result.impact_summary: A single reverting genesis pool blocks all epoch flip operations for every pool, preventing launches, disqualifications, and deposit disabling. Recovery is limited since governance cannot reject pools that have advanced past PRE_LISTING status.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
Both checkAtEpochFlip() and checkBeforeEpochFlip() iterate over all liveNativeTokens in a single transaction, making multiple external calls per pool with no try-catch error isolation. If any single external call reverts for any pool, the entire transaction reverts. This blocks processing of ALL other pools in the batch.

For checkBeforeEpochFlip(), the pre_epoch_period state update occurs only AFTER the loop completes successfully (line ~192), meaning if the loop reverts, the timing guard (block.timestamp >= _period + WEEK) remains satisfied on subsequent calls, but the same revert will occur again - creating a permanent DoS until the problematic pool is somehow resolved.

For checkAtEpochFlip(), pools in PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED state trigger _launchPool() which calls pairFactory.setGenesisStatus(), IGauge.setGenesisPool(), and IGenesisPool.launch() (which internally calls IRouter.addLiquidity with zero slippage). Any revert in this chain blocks all other pool launches.

Critically, governance's only mechanism to remove a pool from liveNativeTokens (rejectGenesisPool) requires the pool to be in NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED or PRE_LISTING status. Once a pool advances to PRE_LAUNCH or PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED, there is NO governance function to remove it from the liveNativeTokens array without it successfully launching or going through the normal disqualification flow.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies the pattern at GenesisPoolManager.sol. The checkAtEpochFlip loop iterates liveNativeTokens making external calls to genesisFactory.getGenesisPool(), IGenesisPool.poolStatus(), IGenesisPool.eligbleForPreLaunchPool(), tokenHandler.whitelistToken(), gaugeManager.createGauge(), IGenesisPool.transferIncentives(), pairFactory.setGenesisStatus(), IGauge.setGenesisPool(), and IGenesisPool.launch() - all without try-catch. The checkBeforeEpochFlip loop similarly calls pairFactory.setGenesisStatus() and IGenesisPool.setPoolStatus() without error isolation, and updates pre_epoch_period only after the loop.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Loop over all liveNativeTokens with multiple external calls per iteration and no try-catch. A single revert in any pool's external calls (e.g., _launchPool -> addLiquidity) blocks the entire function, preventing all other pools from transitioning.

pre_epoch_period is updated only AFTER the loop completes. If any external call in the loop reverts, pre_epoch_period remains stale, and subsequent calls will re-enter the same failing loop. The condition (block.timestamp >= _period + WEEK) keeps being satisfied but the same revert occurs.
Multiple external calls to pairFactory, gauge, and genesis pool (which internally calls router.addLiquidity). Any revert here propagates up to checkAtEpochFlip, blocking all pools.

This is the only governance function to remove a pool from liveNativeTokens, but rejectPool() requires poolStatus == NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED || PRE_LISTING. Pools in PRE_LAUNCH or PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED cannot be rejected, leaving no recovery path for stuck pools.
Status check restricts rejection to early lifecycle states only. Pools that have advanced to PRE_LAUNCH or later cannot be rejected, making the recovery mechanism insufficient for the DoS scenario.

calling_sequence: Function iterates liveNativeTokens in reverse (i from length to 1)
If ANY of these calls revert, the entire checkAtEpochFlip transaction reverts
No pool transitions occur, no pools are removed from liveNativeTokens
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is: checkAtEpochFlip -> loop over liveNativeTokens -> _launchPool -> IGenesisPool.launch -> _addLiquidityAndDistribute -> IRouter.addLiquidity. If addLiquidity reverts for pool N (e.g., due to token behavior, pair state issues, or router problems), the entire transaction reverts. Pools 1 through N-1 that were already processed in this iteration have their state changes rolled back. On subsequent calls, the same revert occurs. Governance cannot use rejectGenesisPool because the stuck pool is in PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED status, which is not accepted by rejectPool(). The only way forward would be upgrading the contract (if behind a proxy), which is a significant intervention.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for GenesisPoolManager initialization, but the vulnerability is architectural and independent of configuration parameters.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation:
Scenario 1 (Griefing): An attacker who gets their token whitelisted by governance creates a genesis pool with a token that has conditional revert behavior (e.g., pausable ERC20). The pool advances through the lifecycle normally. Before the epoch flip that would launch it, the attacker pauses transfers on their token. When checkAtEpochFlip calls _launchPool -> launch -> addLiquidity, the transfer to the router reverts. All other pool launches in the same epoch are blocked.

Scenario 2 (Accidental): A legitimate genesis pool's launch fails due to an unexpected interaction with the pair factory, gauge, or router (e.g., pair already has liquidity from a direct transfer, router contract issue). This blocks all other pool launches.

Scenario 3 (checkBeforeEpochFlip): A pool's external_bribe or gauge was deployed incorrectly. When pairFactory.setGenesisStatus is called for the problematic pool's pair, it reverts. pre_epoch_period never updates, and the function keeps failing on subsequent calls, permanently blocking the deposit-disable mechanism for all pools.

The whitelist requirement adds friction to the attack (governance must approve the token), but doesn't prevent accidental failures or sophisticated attacks where the token appears normal during approval but behaves differently later.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. Impact includes: (1) All pending genesis pool launches are blocked indefinitely, (2) User deposits across all pending pools are locked without the ability to claim refunds (pools aren't set to NOT_QUALIFIED), (3) The epoch flip mechanism is permanently degraded until manual intervention (contract upgrade), (4) For checkBeforeEpochFlip, pre_epoch_period becomes permanently stale, preventing future deposit-disable transitions. Recovery requires either contract upgrade or finding a way to make the failing external call succeed, which may not be possible if the root cause is in an external contract.
recommended_mitigations: Wrap per-pool operations in try-catch blocks within both checkAtEpochFlip and checkBeforeEpochFlip, allowing the loop to continue processing other pools even if one fails
Add a governance function to force-remove a token from liveNativeTokens regardless of pool status (emergency removal)
Add a governance function to set pool status to NOT_QUALIFIED regardless of current status, as a recovery mechanism
Update pre_epoch_period at the beginning of checkBeforeEpochFlip rather than the end, or use a separate tracking mechanism that doesn't depend on loop completion
Consider processing pools individually rather than in batch, with separate entry points per pool
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy GenesisPoolManager, GenesisPoolFactory, a mock PairFactory, mock GaugeManager, mock TokenHandler, and a PausableERC20 token. 2. Whitelist the pausable token and create a genesis pool. 3. Advance the pool through NATIVE_TOKEN_DEPOSITED -> PRE_LISTING -> PRE_LAUNCH -> PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED. 4. Create a second genesis pool with a normal token, advance it to PRE_LAUNCH_DEPOSIT_DISABLED. 5. Pause the first token. 6. Call checkAtEpochFlip() from the epoch controller. 7. Verify the transaction reverts due to the paused token. 8. Verify the second pool also failed to launch. 9. Attempt rejectGenesisPool on the first pool - verify it reverts with 'INS'. 10. Confirm there is no recovery path.
uncertainties: The exact external dependencies (pairFactory, gaugeManager, tokenHandler) are interfaces without provided implementations - their revert behavior is unknown but any revert will trigger the DoS
Whether governance whitelisting process is strict enough to catch all problematic tokens before they enter the system
Whether the epoch controller has retry logic or alternative paths that could partially mitigate the impact
additional_testing_needed: Test with fee-on-transfer tokens to verify addLiquidity reverts when actual received amounts are less than expected
Test with pausable tokens to verify the DoS scenario end-to-end
Test the exact behavior when pairFactory.setGenesisStatus reverts - does this affect checkBeforeEpochFlip similarly?
Test whether upgrading the contract behind a proxy can resolve a stuck state
Test the reverse-iteration array removal pattern for correctness when multiple pools are removed in the same call
